title: Solve Equations with Regression: The ~ Trick
description: Use the Desmos regression symbol (~) to solve equations and simple systems, restrict the search when there is more than one solution, and decide when regression beats graphing.

blocks.html:
Replace = with ~ and watch

Desmos has a hidden superpower: it can solve equations for you. The trick is a symbol called the regression symbol — the tilde ~.

The calculator is open with the line 3n+5~20 already typed. That is the equation \(3n+5=20\) with ~ in place of =. Look underneath the line: Desmos reports a value for \(n\) and an error score labeled RMSE.

Take a moment before you continue: what value of \(n\) did Desmos find, and what is the RMSE?

content.prompt: The regression \(3n+5\sim20\) asked Desmos to solve \(3n+5=20\). What value of \(n\) did it find?
content.hint: Look directly under the regression line in the calculator — Desmos printed the value it found.
content.explanation: Right. Desmos reports \(n=5\), and it checks out: \(3(5)+5=20\).

blocks.html:
What ~ actually tells Desmos

The ~ symbol says: find a value for the unknown letter that makes the two sides match as closely as possible. When an equation has an exact solution, the match is perfect.

RMSE is Desmos's score for how far off the match is. Read it like this:

  • RMSE \(=0\): a perfect match — the value shown really solves the equation.
  • RMSE shown in scientific notation with a big negative exponent, like \(1.4\times10^{-12}\): that is a rounding-dust distance from zero. Treat it as a perfect match too.
  • RMSE that is clearly not small, like \(2.7\): Desmos could not make the sides match — be suspicious of the value it shows.

One rule before you try it on everything: this works for any letter except lowercase \(x\) and \(y\). Desmos reserves those two for graphing. We will deal with that on the next screen.

content.prompt: You type \(7k-3\sim11\) and Desmos reports \(k=2\) with RMSE \(=3.2\times10^{-14}\). What should you conclude?
choices: The match is essentially perfect, so \(k=2\) solves \(7k-3=11\).
The RMSE is not exactly \(0\), so \(k=2\) is not a real solution.
The answer is off by \(3.2\times10^{-14}\), so report a value slightly less than \(2\).
Desmos failed, so you should start over with a different method.
content.hint: How big is \(3.2\times10^{-14}\) written as a decimal? Count the zeros.
content.explanation: Exactly. \(3.2\times10^{-14}\) is \(0.000000000000032\) — rounding dust. An RMSE of \(0\) or a tiny number in scientific notation both mean the regression found a genuine solution.

blocks.html:
When the variable is x (or y)

There is one catch. Desmos reserves lowercase \(x\) and \(y\) for graphing, so a line like 5x-3~12 will not solve anything.

The fix is quick: swap in a letter Desmos is not using. The easiest choice is the capital version of the same letter — capital X for \(x\), capital Y for \(y\). To Desmos, \(X\) and \(x\) are completely different symbols.

The calculator shows 5X-3~12, standing in for \(5x-3=12\). Check the value and the RMSE. Then remember the last step: the question still uses \(x\), so your final answer is \(x=3\), not \(X=3\).

calculator.title: Capital X stands in for x

content.prompt: You want Desmos to solve \(5x-3=12\) for you. Which entry works?
content.hint: Two things must both be true: the = must become ~, and the unknown cannot be a letter Desmos reserves for graphing.
content.explanation: Yes. Capital \(X\) avoids the reserved lowercase \(x\), and ~ tells Desmos to solve. It finds \(X=3\), which you report as \(x=3\).

blocks.html:
The regression move

  1. Rename if needed. If the variable is \(x\) or \(y\), capitalize it: \(X\) or \(Y\).
  2. Swap = for ~. Type the equation otherwise exactly as written — no rearranging needed.
  3. Read the value Desmos reports for the variable.
  4. Check the RMSE. It should be \(0\) or a tiny number in scientific notation.
  5. Answer with the original variable.

Step 2 is what makes this tool so fast. Graphing makes you move everything to one side first; regression takes the equation as-is.

What if the equation has two solutions?

The equation \(m^2=9\) has two solutions: \(3\) and \(-3\). But a regression reports only one — the first solution it happens to find.

Two lines are set up in the calculator. The first is the plain regression m^2~9. The second adds curly braces: m^2~9 {m<0}. The braces tell Desmos to search for a solution only where \(m<0\).

Compare the two results. The plain line shows just one of the two solutions. The restricted line has no choice: it must report \(m=-3\).

content.prompt: The equation \(u^2=25\) has solutions \(5\) and \(-5\). The regression \(u^2\sim25\) reported \(u=5\), but the question needs the negative solution. What should you type?
content.hint: The braces describe where the solution you want lives. Where on the number line is the negative solution?
content.explanation: Right. \(\{u<0\}\) restricts the search to negative numbers, so Desmos must report \(u=-5\). Moving the minus sign into the equation changes the equation itself — \(u^2=-25\) has no real solutions at all.

blocks.html:
Two unknowns? Regression can still do it

Suppose a question gives you a system: \(b=2a+1\) and \(3a+b=16\). Regression can solve it, but Desmos enforces three rules:

  1. Only one line gets a ~. Every other equation must be entered as a definition: one letter alone on the left, like \(b=2a+1\). A line like \(a+b=16\) is not a definition — it does not tell Desmos what any single letter equals, so Desmos cannot use it.
  2. No letter can be defined twice. Two lines that both start with \(b=\) will produce an error.
  3. Pick the right line for the ~. Give the ~ to the equation that is hardest to rearrange into a definition — or, if two equations would define the same letter, make one of those two the regression.

Try it: two equations, one ~

The calculator has the system set up: the definition b=2a+1 and the regression 3a+b~16. Desmos substitutes the definition into the regression and solves for \(a\). Check the value it found.

Now the best part: after a regression, the solution stays stored in the variables. On a new empty line, type b — Desmos shows its value instantly. Then type a+b on another line.

You can keep computing with the solution — exactly what a multi-step SAT problem needs.

content.prompt: You just solved the system: \(a=3\) and \(b=7\), and both values are still stored. You type \(2a+b\) on a new line. What does Desmos show?
content.hint: Desmos still remembers \(a=3\) and \(b=7\). Substitute them into \(2a+b\) — and mind which letter gets doubled.
content.explanation: Yes: \(2(3)+7=13\). Because the regression leaves the solution stored, any follow-up quantity is one line of typing.

content.prompt: A question gives the system \(m+n=12\) and \(m^2-n=18\). Which setup follows the regression rules?
choices: \(n=12-m\) as a definition, and \(m^2-n\sim18\) as the regression
content.hint: Count the ~ symbols: there must be exactly one. Every other line needs a single letter alone on one side, and no letter defined twice.
content.explanation: Right. \(m+n=12\) rearranges easily into the definition \(n=12-m\), so the harder equation gets the ~. Two regressions break the one-~ rule; \(m+n=12\) as written is not a definition Desmos can use; and the last setup defines \(n\) twice and gives Desmos nothing to solve.

blocks.html:
Choosing the right tool

Reach for regression when:

  • You need one solution and any solution will do — regression hands you the first one it finds.
  • You will use the solution in later steps — regression leaves the value stored in the variable, ready for the next line.

Reach for graphing (x-intercepts) instead when:

  • You need all the solutions — for example, "what is the sum of the solutions?" Regression shows only one, and it is easy to forget the others exist.
  • The question is about how many solutions there are — none, one, or two. Counting x-intercepts answers that at a glance; a single regression result cannot.

content.prompt: Which question is the best fit for the regression method?
choices: If \(3(v+4)-2=19\), what is the value of \(6v\)?
What is the sum of all solutions of \(w^2-5w+4=0\)?
For which value of \(c\) does \(x^2+cx+9=0\) have exactly one solution?
How many distinct real solutions does \(z^2+6=0\) have?
content.hint: Regression shines when one solution is enough and you will compute with it afterward.
content.explanation: Right. The first question has a single solution and asks for \(6v\) — regression solves it and keeps \(v\) stored so you can type \(6v\) immediately. The other three need all the solutions or a count of solutions, which is graphing territory.

content.title: Your turn: solve with ~
content.instructions_html:
Solve \(6w-7=2w+9\) using regression.

Type the equation on one line exactly as written, replacing the = with ~. No rearranging needed — that is the whole point.

feedback.success_message_html: That is it — Desmos reports \(w=4\) with RMSE \(0\). Keep that result in mind for the next question.
feedback.retry_message_html: Not yet. Check two things: did you keep both sides exactly as written, and did you put ~ (not =) between them?

attempt_based_hints.message_html: All on one line: type the left side 6w-7, then the ~ symbol, then the right side 2w+9.

feedback.solution_html: The line to enter is 6w-7~2w+9. Desmos finds \(w=4\) with RMSE \(0\).

content.prompt: Your regression found \(w=4\) with RMSE \(0\). The question actually asks: what is the value of \(3w\)?
content.hint: You can type \(3w\) on a new line in the calculator — Desmos still remembers \(w\).
content.explanation: Correct: \(3w=3(4)=12\). Typing \(3w\) directly works because the regression left \(w=4\) stored — no re-solving, no copying decimals by hand.

calculator.title: Let the stored value do the work

content.prompt: Without looking back, which sequence is the regression method for solving an equation?
choices: If the variable is \(x\) or \(y\), capitalize it; replace = with ~; read the value Desmos found; confirm RMSE is \(0\) or tiny; answer with the original variable.
Move everything to one side; graph the expression; click every x-intercept; report the x-values.
Replace the variable with \(x\); replace = with ~; read the y-intercept as the answer.
Replace = with ~ on every equation in the problem; read all the values at once.
content.hint: Think: rename, ~, read, RMSE, translate back.
content.explanation: You have it. Rename \(x\) or \(y\) to a capital, swap = for ~, read the value, confirm the RMSE is essentially zero, and report using the question's original variable.

content.html:
You have a new SAT tool

You can now solve an equation by swapping = for ~, dodge the reserved letters with capital \(X\) and \(Y\), aim the search with braces when there is more than one solution, set up a system as one regression plus definitions, and keep computing with the stored result.

Just remember the trade-off: regression hands you one solution. When a question needs all of them — or a count of them — graph instead.
